claims.claim_text: The opening logistics-company incident is presented as a real January 2024 breach with specific data-loss scope, remediation time, and financial penalties.
claims.span_hint: line 22 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: In January 2024, a logistics company discovered that every pod in their GKE cluster had read/write access to every Cloud Storage bucket and every Pub/Sub topic in their project. A junior developer had deployed a debug pod that scraped all Pub/Sub messages from the production order queue and wrote them to a personal GCS bucket for "testing." The data included customer addresses, phone numbers, and delivery instructions for 2.1 million orders. The root cause was depressingly common: when the cluster was created, the default node service account was granted the `Editor` role on the project, and every pod on the cluster inherited that identity. No one had configured Workload Identity. The remediation cost $890,000 in legal fees, notification costs, and a GDPR fine. The fix---configuring Workload Identity and scoping IAM permissions per pod---took two days.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A common GKE security failure is letting workloads inherit broad node-level Google Cloud permissions. When pods can all use an over-privileged node identity, a debug pod, compromised workload, or careless operator can reach data and services far beyond what that workload should access. Workload Identity and tightly scoped IAM roles are the standard fix.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with precise dates, counts, costs, and remediation timing.

claims.claim_text: Without Workload Identity Federation for GKE, workloads authenticate through the node-level metadata path and can end up using the node's Google Cloud identity.
claims.span_hint: lines 24, 32 | sections: Why This Module Matters; The Problem: Node-Level Identity
claims.rationale: Google's Workload Identity docs describe the metadata-server interception model and the pre-WIF metadata path that workloads use for Google Cloud authentication.

claims.claim_text: Workload Identity Federation for GKE lets you grant Kubernetes workloads fine-grained access to specific Google Cloud APIs without using service account key files.
claims.span_hint: lines 24, 54 | sections: Why This Module Matters; Workload Identity Federation for GKE
claims.rationale: The Workload Identity concept page directly states that WIF grants Kubernetes workloads access to specific APIs without less secure key-file methods.

claims.claim_text: Fleet Workload Identity Federation extends Workload Identity across fleet member clusters, including clusters in different projects.
claims.span_hint: lines 149-151 | section: Fleet Workload Identity Federation (Cross-Project)
claims.rationale: Google's fleet WIF documentation explicitly says the feature extends WIF to fleet member clusters across projects.

claims.claim_text: Binary Authorization uses policy checks and attestations to control which container images can be deployed, and blocks non-compliant deployments while logging the reason.
claims.rationale: The Binary Authorization overview directly documents attestation-based authorization and enforcement with Cloud Audit Logs.

claims.claim_text: The Binary Authorization Friday-to-Monday outage anecdote is presented as a real incident with a precise 48-hour impact window.
claims.anchor_text: **War Story**: A team enabled Binary Authorization in enforce mode on a Friday afternoon. On Monday morning, their CI/CD pipeline had broken because Cloud Build was pushing images but not creating attestations. Every deployment for 48 hours was blocked. Start with `DRYRUN_AUDIT_LOG_ONLY` mode to identify what would be blocked before switching to enforce mode.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **War Story**: Teams that switch Binary Authorization straight to enforce mode can break deployments if their build pipeline is not yet producing the required attestations. Start with `DRYRUN_AUDIT_LOG_ONLY` mode so you can see what would be blocked before enforcing.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed operational incident with a specific timeline.

claims.claim_text: Binary Authorization dry-run mode allows violating images to deploy but records policy-violation details in Cloud Audit Logs until enforcement is enabled.
claims.rationale: The dry-run guide explicitly says violating images are allowed and that compliance status is logged until enforcement is turned on.

claims.claim_text: Shielded GKE Nodes provide strong, verifiable node identity and integrity and are enabled by default on new Autopilot and Standard clusters.
claims.rationale: The Shielded GKE Nodes guide states both the default enablement and the node identity/integrity goal.

claims.claim_text: Shielded node protections are built from Secure Boot, vTPM-backed measured boot, and integrity monitoring against a known-good baseline.
claims.rationale: The Shielded VM overview documents Secure Boot, vTPM measured boot, and integrity monitoring with baseline comparison.

claims.claim_text: Confidential GKE Nodes protect data in use with hardware-based memory encryption and support AMD SEV as one Confidential Computing technology.
claims.rationale: The current GKE Confidential Nodes docs directly describe hardware-based memory encryption and list AMD SEV among supported technologies.

claims.claim_text: The module states that physical access or a cold-boot attack categorically cannot read Confidential Node memory.
claims.anchor_text: Even if an attacker has physical access to the server or can perform a cold-boot attack, they cannot read the node's memory.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This is designed to protect data in use with hardware-backed memory encryption and hardware isolation.
claims.rationale: The cited Google docs support data-in-use protection but do not directly back this categorical cold-boot wording for the GKE-specific AMD SEV presentation.

claims.claim_text: The table implies Confidential Nodes always use AMD SEV specifically for memory encryption.
claims.span_hint: line 353 | section: Confidential Nodes comparison table
claims.suggested_rewrite: Yes, using the configured Confidential Computing technology
claims.rationale: Current GKE docs support multiple Confidential Computing technologies, not only AMD SEV.

claims.claim_text: The table gives a specific Confidential Nodes performance overhead of roughly 2-6%.
claims.span_hint: line 354 | section: Confidential Nodes comparison table
claims.rationale: The current Google docs describe performance impact as workload-dependent rather than backing this fixed percentage range.

claims.claim_text: The table says Confidential Nodes are N2D-only.
claims.span_hint: line 355 | section: Confidential Nodes comparison table
claims.suggested_rewrite: machine types that support the chosen Confidential Computing technology
claims.rationale: Current GKE docs support multiple technologies and supported machine types vary by technology.

claims.claim_text: The table gives a specific Confidential Nodes pricing premium of about 10%.
claims.span_hint: line 356 | section: Confidential Nodes comparison table
claims.suggested_rewrite: additional cost that depends on the selected machine type and Confidential Computing technology
claims.rationale: This dated pricing-style percentage is not directly backed by the current official docs and is likely to change over time.

claims.claim_text: The security posture dashboard provides centralized visibility into security concerns across multiple GKE clusters and workloads.
claims.span_hint: line 365 | section: GKE Security Posture Dashboard
claims.rationale: Google's dashboard overview says it automates detection and reporting across multiple clusters and workloads.

claims.claim_text: The module says the current dashboard scans for misconfigurations, vulnerability exposure, and policy violations as a built-in capability bundle.
claims.span_hint: line 365 | section: GKE Security Posture Dashboard
claims.anchor_text: It scans for misconfigurations, vulnerability exposure, and policy violations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: It surfaces configuration concerns from GKE posture features and findings from integrated security products.
claims.rationale: As of April 23, 2026, Google documents the built-in feature set differently and notes that GKE workload vulnerability scanning tiers have been deprecated or removed.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes security posture scanning audits workload configuration concerns such as privileged containers and runAsNonRoot violations.
claims.span_hint: lines 384, 392 | sections: What It Detects; Hardening Pod Security
claims.rationale: The configuration-scanning doc explicitly lists privileged-container and runAsNonRoot checks.

claims.claim_text: The bullet says the dashboard checks for CVEs in Artifact Registry images as a current built-in feature.
claims.anchor_text: - **Container vulnerabilities**: CVEs in container images from Artifact Registry
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Container vulnerabilities**: use the current Google Cloud vulnerability-scanning products that are available in your environment, rather than assuming this remains a built-in GKE standard posture check
claims.rationale: Google's 2026 docs note that the older GKE workload vulnerability scanning tiers were deprecated and removed on published dates.

claims.claim_text: The bullet says the dashboard checks for unauthenticated internet exposure as a built-in posture finding.
claims.anchor_text: - **Network exposure**: Services exposed to the internet without authentication
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Network exposure**: review internet exposure with the specific networking and security products you have enabled
claims.rationale: The cited GKE posture docs do not directly document this as a current built-in GKE posture check.

claims.claim_text: The bullet says the dashboard broadly checks for overly permissive ClusterRoleBindings.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **RBAC issues**: certain risky bindings, such as grants to effectively anonymous users, can be surfaced
claims.rationale: Google's configuration-scanning docs document specific RBAC findings rather than this broader blanket statement.

claims.claim_text: The bullet says the dashboard checks for images not from trusted registries as a built-in supply-chain control.
claims.anchor_text: - **Supply chain**: Images not from trusted registries
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Supply chain**: image trust should be enforced with dedicated controls such as Binary Authorization and related supply-chain tooling
claims.rationale: The current GKE posture docs do not directly support this as a built-in dashboard concern category.

claims.claim_text: GKE supports Pod Security Standards through the PodSecurity admission controller, including enforce, audit, and warn modes.
claims.rationale: The GKE PodSecurity guide directly documents the controller, the PSS levels, and enforce/audit/warn behavior.

claims.claim_text: The Secret Manager add-on integrates GKE with Secret Manager and mounts secrets into Pods as files using a CSI-based mechanism.
claims.rationale: The Secret Manager add-on docs explicitly describe mounted secret volumes for GKE Pods.

claims.claim_text: Using the Secret Manager add-on keeps secret material in Secret Manager rather than syncing it into Kubernetes Secret objects by default.
claims.span_hint: lines 513-515 | section: Mounting Secrets in Pods
claims.rationale: The add-on docs say secrets are mounted from Secret Manager and that Sync as Kubernetes Secret isn't supported by this add-on.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes Secrets are stored unencrypted in etcd by default unless encryption at rest is configured.
claims.span_hint: lines 521-523, 553 | sections: Secret Manager vs Kubernetes Secrets; Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes Secrets docs explicitly warn that Secrets are stored unencrypted in etcd by default.

claims.claim_text: Secret Manager provides version history and supports listing older versions for rollback and auditing use cases.
claims.span_hint: line 523 | section: Secret Manager vs Kubernetes Secrets
claims.rationale: Google's Secret Manager docs explicitly describe listing versions over time and rolling back to older versions.

claims.claim_text: The Secret Manager add-on supports managed rotation with a configurable rotation interval.
claims.span_hint: line 524 | section: Secret Manager vs Kubernetes Secrets
claims.rationale: The add-on docs include enable-secret-manager-rotation and rotation-interval support.

claims.claim_text: Secret Manager access events are captured in Cloud Audit Logs, including AccessSecretVersion data-access events.
claims.span_hint: line 525 | section: Secret Manager vs Kubernetes Secrets
claims.rationale: The Secret Manager audit logging reference explicitly documents AccessSecretVersion audit logging.

claims.claim_text: Secret Manager access control is IAM-based and can be granted at the secret level rather than only within a namespace.
claims.span_hint: line 527 | section: Secret Manager vs Kubernetes Secrets
claims.rationale: The IAM access-control page documents Secret Manager roles and the lowest-level resource scope as Secret.

claims.claim_text: Did You Know item 1 uses an unsupported internal 2019 audit statistic and over-precise historical framing about pre-Workload-Identity practices.
claims.anchor_text: 1. **Before Workload Identity existed, the recommended workaround was to distribute service account JSON key files as Kubernetes Secrets.** This meant private key material was stored in etcd, potentially logged, and visible to anyone with RBAC access to the namespace. Google internal security audits in 2019 found that 34% of GKE clusters in a sample had service account keys stored as Kubernetes Secrets. Workload Identity, launched in 2019, eliminated the need for key files entirely by using short-lived, automatically-rotated tokens.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **Before Workload Identity became available, teams often used service account key files or other credential-distribution workarounds.** That approach put long-lived credentials closer to workloads. Workload Identity removed the need to hand service account key files to Pods by using short-lived credentials instead.
claims.rationale: The internal audit percentage is not attributable from allowlisted primary sources, and the full item mixes that unsupported statistic with over-precise historical framing.

claims.claim_text: Binary Authorization attestations are tied to the image digest rather than the tag, so replacing a tag points to different content and the original attestation no longer matches.
claims.rationale: The Binary Authorization overview states that attestations contain the image registry path and digest and are verified at deploy time.

claims.claim_text: Did You Know item 3 makes unsupported hardware-specific claims and gives a fixed 2-6% performance range for Confidential GKE Nodes.
claims.anchor_text: 3. **Confidential GKE Nodes encrypt each node's memory with a unique key that changes on every boot.** The key is generated inside the AMD Secure Processor and never leaves the CPU. Google's hypervisor, host OS, and other VMs on the same physical host cannot read the node's memory. The performance overhead is typically 2-6% for most workloads because the encryption happens in the CPU's memory controller at hardware speed, not in software.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Confidential GKE Nodes use hardware-based memory encryption to protect data in use.** The exact protections, supported machine types, and performance impact depend on the Confidential Computing technology and workload you choose.
claims.rationale: Current allowlisted docs support the general data-in-use protection model but not this full bundle of specific hardware assertions and performance numbers.

claims.claim_text: The GKE metadata server intercepts pod traffic to the standard metadata endpoint, uses STS token exchange for workload credentials, and returns 1-hour tokens by default.
claims.rationale: The Workload Identity concept page documents the metadata endpoint interception, STS exchange, and 1-hour default token lifetime.

claims.claim_text: The module says pods without Workload Identity receive a permission-denied response instead of node credentials.
claims.anchor_text: Pods without Workload Identity receive a "permission denied" response instead of the node's credentials.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Pods without the necessary Workload Identity configuration do not get direct access to the Compute Engine metadata server through this path.
claims.rationale: The official docs clearly document metadata interception but do not directly back this exact permission-denied wording.

claims.claim_text: By default, GKE uses the Compute Engine default service account as the node service account unless you configure a custom node service account.
claims.rationale: The GKE service-account docs explicitly state the default node-service-account behavior and recommend a custom minimally privileged node SA.

claims.claim_text: When using the linked-IAM-service-account method, both the iam.workloadIdentityUser IAM binding and the iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account annotation are required.
claims.rationale: Google's Workload Identity how-to explicitly says both the IAM allow policy and the annotation are required for this method.

claims.claim_text: The module recommends reviewing Binary Authorization dry-run logs for 1-2 weeks before enforcing.
claims.anchor_text: Start with `DRYRUN_AUDIT_LOG_ONLY` mode; review logs for 1-2 weeks before enforcing
claims.suggested_rewrite: Start with `DRYRUN_AUDIT_LOG_ONLY` mode; review logs long enough to identify legitimate deployments before enforcing
claims.rationale: Dry-run mode is documented, but the specific 1-2 week duration is a teaching recommendation rather than a primary-source fact.

further_reading.title: Workload Identity Federation for GKE

further_reading.title: Binary Authorization Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary reference for attestations, deployment enforcement, and audit-log behavior.

further_reading.title: Shielded GKE Nodes
further_reading.why_relevant: Canonical GKE guide for node identity and integrity protections.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Security Posture Scanning
further_reading.why_relevant: Best source for what GKE posture scanning actually checks in current releases.

further_reading.title: Secret Manager Add-on for GKE
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary doc for mounting externally managed secrets into Pods and for rotation support.

notes: Several security-posture claims are outdated as of April 23, 2026. Google documents that the older GKE workload vulnerability scanning tiers were deprecated and removed on published dates, so those claims should be revised rather than cited as current behavior.
